    Home › Types › Narrowbody airliners

    Narrowbody airliners

    One aisle, and the aircraft you actually fly on most.

    Aircraft here9
    Longest57.12 m
    Combined built25,992
    First flights1958–2017
    What it means

    The category, explained

    A narrowbody has a single aisle — a fuselage roughly 3 to 4 metres wide. They are the workhorses of aviation: the vast majority of flights taken worldwide every day are on single-aisle aircraft, and the 737 alone has been built more than any other jet airliner in history.

    They rarely top the size charts, but the stretched ones sneak on: the Boeing 757-300, at 54.47 metres, is the longest single-aisle airliner ever built — a remarkably thin pencil of an aircraft carrying up to 243 people.

    The aircraft

    Every narrowbody airliner here

    Silhouettes are sized by real wingspan — bigger aircraft draw bigger.

    About the data. Every specification is compiled from manufacturer documents and cited per row. Operator fleet counts are approximate snapshots, marked ≈ and dated; individually verified figures carry a ✓. All silhouettes are original works of aircraft.fyi, drawn to true scale. Corrections and enquiries: business@luck.fyi — we fix errors visibly, not silently.
